---
title: "Laszlo Szabo"
subtitle: "Consultant Transplant Surgeon"
description: "Laszlo Szabo is a Consultant Transplant Surgeon at Cardiff Transplant Unit, specialising in kidney and pancreas transplantation, organ utilisation, and clinical data science."
image: images/profile_pic.jpeg
image-alt: "Photo of Laszlo Szabo, Consultant Transplant Surgeon"
format:
  html:
    pagetitle: "Consultant Transplant Surgeon, Cardiff"
    include-in-header: assets/schema-person.html
about:
  template: trestles
  image-shape: round
  image-width: 18em
  links:
    - icon: twitter
      text: X / Twitter
      href: https://x.com/drszabolaszlo
    - icon: linkedin
      text: LinkedIn
      href: https://www.linkedin.com/in/drszabolaszlo/
    - icon: github
      text: GitHub
      href: https://github.com/Laszlo75
    - text: "{{< fa brands orcid >}} ORCID"
      href: https://orcid.org/0000-0002-6881-1542
    - icon: envelope
      text: Contact
      href: mailto:laszlo.szabo@wales.nhs.uk
toc: false
---

I am a Consultant Transplant Surgeon at the **Cardiff Transplant Unit** with a clinical and research focus on kidney and pancreas transplantation, organ retrieval, organ utilisation, and perioperative outcomes.

I lead initiatives aimed at improving access to transplantation, and optimising post-transplant care. I collaborate with national programmes to improve organ utilisation policy and contribute to multicentre clinical studies.

## Research Interests

::: {.research-pills}
[Kidney and pancreas transplantation outcomes]{.pill}
[Organ preservation and machine perfusion]{.pill}
[Donor risk stratification and organ utilisation]{.pill}
[Vascular access for haemodialysis]{.pill}
[Data science applied to transplant decision-making]{.pill}
:::

## Current Work

::: {.highlight-block}
I helped establish and now run the **Normothermic Regional Perfusion (NRP) programme** at Cardiff, improving organ quality assessment and expanding the use of DCD donor organs for transplantation.

I am increasingly interested in applying R and data science methods to clinical transplant questions — from survival modelling and risk prediction to improving how we use national organ offering data.
:::
